identity.name: Bloor Street West (The Annex section)
identity.address: Bloor Street West, The Annex, Toronto, ON

discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). One of the few North American streets that earns a mention — the Annex section from Spadina to Bathurst has the mixed use, scale, and continuous active edges that Jacobs finds absent in most of the continent.

narrative.summary: One of the few North American streets that earns a mention — the Annex section from Spadina to Bathurst has the mixed use, scale, and continuous active edges that Jacobs finds absent in most of the continent.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: Victorian commercial buildings maintained at human scale, independent shops and restaurants at ground floor, apartments above, subway creating foot traffic, mature street trees. Worth walking as a local example of what the book is about — in your own city.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: BMV Books
blocks.address: 471 Bloor St W, Toronto, ON M5S 1X9, Canada
blocks.why: A massive used bookstore directly on Bloor's active edge. It shows the kind of dense, low-rise cultural frontage Jacobs values, where browsing spills pedestrian energy back onto the sidewalk.

blocks.name: Hot Docs Ted Rogers Cinema
blocks.address: 506 Bloor St W, Toronto, ON M5S 1Y3, Canada
blocks.why: An institutional anchor right on the corridor. The marquee, festival programming, and constant foot traffic give Bloor the cultural rhythm that makes a great street feel inhabited beyond shopping hours.

blocks.name: Seekers Books
blocks.address: 509 Bloor St W, Toronto, ON M5S 1Y2, Canada
blocks.why: A tightly packed occult and secondhand bookshop beside cafés and cinemas. This is Annex Bloor at its most fine-grained, proving how variety and small storefronts keep the block visually and socially alive.
